We are building Supacomp, a spatial, AI-native web builder that allows developers and founders to create websites and apps visually on an infinite canvas, powered by LLMs like GPT-4. We are not just another drag-and-drop tool. We are rethinking how humans and AI work together to build software.  
Most AI coding tools add AI to existing workflows as an afterthought. Supacomp is designed from the ground up around AI. Every component exists as a node on a canvas. This setup gives AI the complete spatial context of your entire app architecture, not just the file you have open. This approach provides LLMs the context they need to generate accurate, non-breaking code.  
A node-based visual architecture system where pages and components connect as graph nodes  .
Surgical AI editing, allowing you to isolate a component, prompt changes, and generate variants side-by-side without affecting the rest of the site  .
 Spatial Versioning with CRDT-based real-time collaboration and one-click A/B deployment  
Production-ready React + Tailwind + Next.js export with no vendor lock-in  .
Real-time HMR previews directly on the canvas  .
What we want to build at this hackathon:  
We want to dive deeper into OpenAI's models within the spatial layer. We aim to use GPT-4's reasoning not only to generate components but also to understand their relationships. We want it to suggest architectural improvements, catch inconsistencies across pages, and refactor entire site sections safely. Essentially, we want an AI that thinks about your app like a senior architect would. We are tackling a real problem — the disconnect between design, development, and AI tools costs teams a lot of time and money. Supacomp brings all three together on one canvas. We have already validated the concept with a waitlist, built a working studio interface, and understand how OpenAI's capabilities can elevate this product.  
We do not just use AI as a feature. AI is central to our product. This hackathon provides the perfect opportunity to demonstrate what that truly means.

I believe I’m a strong fit for this hackathon because I don’t just build projects—I design end-to-end, production-oriented systems with a focus on real-world usability, scalability, and automation.

From my past work and explorations, I’ve consistently worked at the intersection of AI, automation, and backend systems. For example, I’ve been actively building solutions involving agentic workflows using APIs like Claude, browser automation tools like Playwright, and scraping frameworks such as Firecrawl. My goal in these systems is not just to “make something work,” but to create adaptive pipelines that can handle dynamic user requirements across multiple websites and workflows.

A key aspect that sets me apart is my focus on system design thinking rather than isolated features. Whether it’s:

Designing automated form-filling agents that can generalize across platforms
Building image similarity and face detection pipelines using perceptual hashing and modern CV techniques
Structuring backend systems with FastAPI, JWT authentication, and scalable email infrastructure (SMTP, Resend, etc.)

I approach problems with a mindset of modularity, extensibility, and real deployment readiness.

I’ve also explored advanced AI topics like:

Trade-offs in RAG systems and retrieval pipelines
Fine-tuning strategies (e.g., DINOv2 architecture considerations)
Running and optimizing models on constrained hardware

This shows my ability to go beyond surface-level implementation and think deeply about performance, architecture, and long-term maintainability.

What I would bring to this hackathon:

The ability to quickly translate ideas into working prototypes
A strong bias toward automation and agent-based systems
Experience integrating multiple tools into cohesive workflows
A focus on building something that is not just impressive technically, but useful and extensible after the hackathon

1-A retail promotion platform that extracts and structures flyer data using YOLO + OpenAI APIs

We're the founding team behind Supacomp. a visual build platform where AI agents generate, patch, and deploy production code directly from a canvas, without the user ever switching tools. We've already built what most hackathon teams would pitch as an idea. We're not here to learn how agents work. We're here because we've been building agentic systems for months and a focused hackathon is the fastest way to push the frontier further. We're specifically drawn to the Agentic Coding and UX for Agentic Applications tracks. both sit directly inside what we build every day.
One canvas. AI design and code. and ships it. That's Supacomp. And that's what we'll be building on, around, or beyond. whichever direction the room needs most.

We are building Supacomp, a spatial, AI-native web builder that allows developers and founders to create websites and apps visually on an infinite canvas, powered by LLMs like GPT-4. We are not just another drag-and-drop tool. We are rethinking how humans and AI work together to build software.  
Most AI coding tools add AI to existing workflows as an afterthought. Supacomp is designed from the ground up around AI. Every component exists as a node on a canvas. This setup gives AI the complete spatial context of your entire app architecture, not just the file you have open. This approach provides LLMs the context they need to generate accurate, non-breaking code.  
A node-based visual architecture system where pages and components connect as graph nodes  .
Surgical AI editing, allowing you to isolate a component, prompt changes, and generate variants side-by-side without affecting the rest of the site  .
 Spatial Versioning with CRDT-based real-time collaboration and one-click A/B deployment  
Production-ready React + Tailwind + Next.js export with no vendor lock-in  .
Real-time HMR previews directly on the canvas  .
What we want to build at this hackathon:  
We want to dive deeper into OpenAI's models within the spatial layer. We aim to use GPT-4's reasoning not only to generate components but also to understand their relationships. We want it to suggest architectural improvements, catch inconsistencies across pages, and refactor entire site sections safely. Essentially, we want an AI that thinks about your app like a senior architect would. We are tackling a real problem — the disconnect between design, development, and AI tools costs teams a lot of time and money. Supacomp brings all three together on one canvas. We have already validated the concept with a waitlist, built a working studio interface, and understand how OpenAI's capabilities can elevate this product.  
We do not just use AI as a feature. AI is central to our product. This hackathon provides the perfect opportunity to demonstrate what that truly means.

So basically I’m Afnan, a curious kid turned developer and all of it self taught. I started my journey 7 years ago when I was 12, fall of 2018 to be exact. For the first two years I did little bits of development here and there. Basic web stuff, Minecraft plugins, GTA servers, the usual “I have a computer and no adult supervision” arc.

Then I got into cloud hosting and game server hosting, and made my very first shell tool called petrovm around 2022 or 2023, right when GPT 3.0 was the new shiny thing. I built the tool completely by myself with zero AI help. What it did was reverse access any Docker based container using proot. You know how Docker is supposed to run one major process per instance. My tool said nah and ran as many processes as you wanted in one single environment. It boomed with around 70 stars and I didn’t even advertise it. It was just my personal thing.

Long story short, some guy used it to DDoS the US government. I was 15 when that happened. I got scared to my bones and archived the repo that same week. That was my first serious project and it took like two years of updates and late nights.

After that I jumped into the world of AI. Built a cancer detection pipeline at 16, then built a bunch of tools on top of the ChatGPT API, Claude API, the usual suspects. I even built my own custom TCN because why use someone else’s brain when you can overengineer your own.

Right now I’m spread across IoT, robotics, AI, web dev, and Android dev with 70 plus repositories. Not forks, my personal junkyard. Around 40 of them are open source. My serious projects include fang, an open source HDL I’m building on top of Rust that currently only does arithmetic operations. Big dreams start with small math. Then there is my tiny tetris os which does exactly what it sounds like, it plays Tetris. Peak operating system design.

I also built a lot of other stuff. My main SaaS was meditech, an all in one hospital to medical store pipeline. Then I built acadly, a marketplace for college students to sell and buy used stuff like calculators with real time SSE chat and public profiles for ratings. After that came co-op, an open source business support system. Think openclaw but for businesses. It has four MCPs for competitor, investor, legal and finance, all connected A2A. It does influencer analysis, personalized mass mail, and I even slapped an LLM council in there before it became a trend on Twitter.

Currently I’m on the verge of dropping out of my second year of engineering to focus on two startups. One is avolta, a real time disaster prevention and urban safety system based on our patented mesh system that fully works without internet or electricity because solar is free real estate. The other is nyvra, a medical wellness platform and companion app with four layer TCN AI models that run completely offline, blockchain backed only if you ever need to connect your data to a doctor, plus a real time hospital map that works like Uber but for patients. We’re also making our own wearable with a custom PCB and the prototype is already cooking.

So yeah, that’s me. A 19 year old curious builder who accidentally became a minor federal side character at 15 and decided to keep shipping anyway.

I believe I’m a strong fit for this hackathon because I focus on solving one of the hardest real-world AI problems — converting unstructured data into structured, queryable intelligence using LLM-driven pipelines.

I’ve been working on systems that take unstructured sources like PDFs and documents and transform them into knowledge graphs, enabling deeper reasoning beyond traditional retrieval. My work includes building pipelines for PDF → entity extraction → normalization → graph construction, and then layering LLM capabilities on top.

I’ve also explored Text-to-Cypher generation, allowing natural language queries to be converted into graph queries, and implemented GraphRAG-style workflows where LLMs interact with structured graph data instead of relying only on vector search. This improves accuracy, reduces hallucinations, and enables multi-hop reasoning.

In addition, I’ve worked on optimizing LLM workflows, especially around token efficiency and cost-performance tradeoffs, which is critical when building scalable AI systems.

What sets me apart is that I don’t just use LLM APIs — I design systems around them. In a hackathon, I can quickly build a working pipeline that goes from messy real-world data to a structured, queryable, and demo-ready AI system with clear practical value.
